nameMeanings.name: Ryan
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Irish Brendan, John Patrick, Dermot, Liam, Paddy, Donovan, Eamon, Eamonn, Kieran, Parnell, Aileen, Brennan.

Irish: shortened form of O'Ryan , an Anglicized form of Ó Riaghain (modern Irish Ó Riain) ‘descendant of Riaghan’, a personal name probably based on the word rí ‘king’. Ryan is one of the commonest surnames in Ireland, though there has been considerable confusion with Regan .

variants.name: Ryon
meanings: Perhaps an altered form of Irish   Ryan  .

variants.name: Ryans
meanings: Irish: variant of Ryan with English excrescent -s.

variants.name: Mulryan
meanings: Irish: Anglicized form of Gaelic Ó Maoilriaghain ‘descendant of the devotee of (Saint) Riaghan’, a personal name probably based on the word rí ‘king’. The family were chieftains in Tipperary and Limerick. The name has sometimes been abbreviated to Ryan , which however can be the Anglicized form of two other Irish surnames.

variants.name: O'Ryan
meanings: (Leinster): Anglicized form of Gaelic Ó Riagháin (modern Irish Ó Riain) ‘descendant of Riaghán’, a personal name probably based on the word rí ‘king’. See Ryan .
(Munster): Anglicized form of Gaelic Ó Maoilriaghain ‘descendant of Maoilriaghain’, a personal name meaning ‘follower of Riaghán’. See Ryan .

variants.name: Ryen
meanings: Norwegian: habitational name from any of more than ten farmsteads, originally named with Old Norse rugr ‘rye’ + vin ‘meadow’.
Possibly also an altered form of Irish   Ryan  .

nameMeanings.name: Thomas
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: New Testament name, borne by one of Christ's twelve apostles, referred to as ‘Thomas, called Didymus’ (John 11:16; 20:24). Didymos is the Greek word for ‘twin’, and the name is the Greek form of an Aramaic byname meaning ‘twin’. The given name has always been popular throughout Christendom, in part because St Thomas's doubts have made him seem a very human character.

nameMeanings.name: Jefferson
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Transferred use of the surname, originally a patronymic meaning ‘son of Jeffrey’. The given name is still sometimes so used. In the United States it has often been bestowed in honour of the statesman  Thomas Jefferson  (  1743–1826 ), principal author of the Declaration of Independence, who became the third president of the Union. He is also remembered as a scientist, architect, and writer.

historicEvents.title: Wilderness Road Opens to Wagons
historicEvents.description: In 1796, the Wilderness Road opened up for wagon use. The route was used by colonial and early settlers to reach Kentucky from the East. It started in Virginia, and went southward to Tennessee and then went north to Kentucky. The main danger of this route was Native American attacks.

historicEvents.title: Movement to Washington D.C.
historicEvents.description: While the growth of the new nation was exponential, the United States didn’t have permanent location to house the Government. The First capital was temporary in New York City but by the second term of George Washington the Capital moved to Philadelphia for the following 10 years. Ultimately during the Presidency of John Adams, the Capital found a permanent home in the District of Columbia.

historicEvents.title: Kentucky Bend Created
historicEvents.description: During the New Madrid earthquakes of 1811-1812, the Kentucky Bend or New Madrid Bend was created. It is located in the southwestern corner of Kentucky on the banks of the Mississippi River.
historicEvents.urlTitle: RootsRated-The Fascinating Story of Kentucky Bend: A Barely-Inhabited Isolated Piece of the State Cut-Off by a Curve in the Mississippi River

historicEvents.title: Louisville and Portland Canal Opens
historicEvents.description: The Louisville and Portland canal opened in 1830. It was a 2 mile canal. It helped with the barrier caused by the Falls of the Ohio River at Louisville by making a route around them.
historicEvents.urlTitle: Triumph at the Falls: The Louisville and Portland Canal

historicEvents.title: The Massive Internal Improvements Act
historicEvents.description: The Massive Internal Improvements Act of 1836 loaned Indiana $10,000,000 to create infrastructure such as canals, railroads, and roads across the state. The act was signed by Whig Governor Noah Noble and passed by the Indiana General Assembly. However, the financial crisis known as the Panic of 1837 thwarted these plans as costs ballooned. Construction on the infrastructure was not completed and the state debt rapidly increased.

historicEvents.title: Indiana Nears Bankruptcy
historicEvents.description: The State of Indiana was near bankruptcy in 1841 due to the inability to repay interest incurred for the Massive Internal Improvement Act. The state liquidated much of its public works. Many of the projects were handed over to the state’s creditors as a way to reduce debt. Only two of the eight proposed infrastructure projects were completed by the creditors.

historicEvents.title: 8th Most Populated State
historicEvents.description: According to the 1850 census Kentucky was the 8th most populated state with 982,405 people.

en.lifeSummary: Thomas Jefferson Ryan was born in 1795, in Kentucky, United States as the son of Thomas Rion. He had at least 2 sons and 2 daughters with Cynthia Nolan. He lived in Charlestown Township, Clark, Indiana, United States in 1850. He died on 17 October 1852, at the age of 57.
